name: Haida Nation
languageFamily: Haida (language isolate — not related to any other language family)
traditionalTerritories: Haida Gwaii (Queen Charlotte Islands) and the southern portion of the Alexander Archipelago in Alaska (including Old Kasaan and portions of Prince of Wales Island). The Haida Nation's territory crosses the US-Canada border.
culturalNotes: The Haida Nation occupies Haida Gwaii — a remote archipelago off the BC coast sometimes called 'the Galapagos of the North' for its biodiversity. The Haida language is a language isolate with no known relatives — linguistically as isolated as Basque. Haida formline design art is one of the most recognized Indigenous art traditions in North America; this recognition creates both opportunity and risk. Two distinct Haida communities exist: Old Massett (on the northern island) and Skidegate (on the southern island), each with distinct dialects and cultural details. Haida Nation also includes the Kaigani Haida in Alaska (Old Kasaan). The Haida Nation has been internationally prominent in environmental activism and cultural sovereignty assertion, including landmark legal cases.
heritageSkillRelevance: Dugout canoe construction — Haida ocean-going canoes were among the largest on the Northwest Coast, traded throughout the region
Formline design art — the two-dimensional art system (Level 2 for foundational design principles; Level 3 for family-specific crest designs)
Totem pole carving — family and clan crests are Level 2-3; publicly documented carving techniques are Level 1
Red and yellow cedar harvesting and working
Halibut fishing with traditional hooks — a central Haida food source
Xaad Kil language revitalization (collaboration between Massett and Skidegate communities)
Argillite carving — a Haida-specific material found only on Haida Gwaii
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Haida formline design is owned by families and clans — specific crest designs (raven, eagle, bear, killer whale, etc.) belong to specific lineages. Level 2 for publicly documented foundational design principles; Level 3 for family-specific crests. Content builders must not reproduce specific crest designs without documented permission. Distinguish Old Massett and Skidegate communities — each has distinct cultural and dialect details. The Haida Nation's US (Kaigani Haida/Alaska) and Canadian (BC) populations are one people — the colonial border does not divide the Haida Nation.

name: Tlingit
traditionalTerritories: Southeast Alaska coastal region (US) and the northern BC coast (Canada). Traditional territory spans from Yakutat Bay in the north to the Portland Canal in the south, encompassing the Alexander Archipelago, Lynn Canal, and the adjacent coast. Cross-border US-Canada territory without taking sovereignty positions on either side.
culturalNotes: The Tlingit occupy the largest geographic range of any Northwest Coast people — their territory spans the entirety of Southeast Alaska (US) and the adjacent BC coast (Canada). Tlingit is a Na-Dene language, related distantly to Athabascan languages of the interior, making Tlingit linguistically distinct from their Haida and Tsimshian neighbors. Tlingit society is organized into two moieties (Raven and Eagle/Wolf) with multiple clans under each. The Tlingit were major participants in the Pacific maritime fur trade, which transformed their economy and material culture in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The Chilkat Tlingit (of Klukwan, Alaska) maintain one of the most elaborate weaving traditions in North America — the Chilkat blanket.
heritageSkillRelevance: Chilkat blanket weaving — Klukwan-based tradition; one of the most technically complex weaving forms in North America (design protocols are Level 2-3 for family-owned patterns)
Totem pole carving — clan crest system; foundation techniques are Level 1; specific crest designs are Level 2-3 family property
Dugout canoe construction for Southeast Alaska and BC fjord navigation
Tlingit language learning resources (multiple Tlingit communities have programs in both US and Canada)
Copper working — Tlingit coppers (shield-shaped wealth objects) are central to potlatch wealth systems
Salmon and halibut fishing in Southeast Alaska and BC waters
Mountain goat wool and Raven's Tail weaving tradition (older than Chilkat)
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Tlingit territory crosses the US-Canada border — when discussing Tlingit heritage, do not assume US or Canadian jurisdiction defines cultural identity. The colonial border does not divide the Tlingit people. Clan crest designs carry family ownership at Level 2-3. Chilkat blanket patterns are family property at Level 3. Ceremonial songs and protocols are Level 3-4. The module does not take political positions on either US or Canadian sovereignty over the region.

name: Gitxsan (Gitxsan)
alternateNames: People of the River of Mist
language: Gitxsanimx (Gitksan — a Tsimshianic language distinct from Sm'algyax)
traditionalTerritories: Upper Skeena River watershed in northern BC, from Hazelton upstream through the upper Skeena canyon and river system. Also called the Skeena River above the Tsimshian territory.
culturalNotes: The Gitxsan are a river people whose territories are in the upper Skeena River watershed — a salmon-rich interior river environment connected to the coast via the Skeena corridor. The Gitxsan were one of the two nations at the center of the landmark Delgamuukw v. British Columbia Supreme Court case (1997), which established that Aboriginal title had not been extinguished in BC and that oral histories are admissible as evidence in Canadian courts. Delgamuukw is one of the most significant legal decisions in Canadian Aboriginal law.
heritageSkillRelevance: Upper Skeena River salmon fishing — significant chinook, coho, and sockeye runs in the upper Skeena
Gitxsanimx language revitalization efforts
Dugout and river canoe construction for upper Skeena River travel
Interior forest plant knowledge — the upper Skeena has distinct boreal-transitional ecology
Trade network connections — upper Skeena corridor between coast and interior
Totem pole traditions — Gitxsan poles are documented in museum collections; family and clan ownership applies
Oral history as legal evidence — the Delgamuukw case context is publicly documented (Level 1)
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Gitxsan is a Tsimshianic language — distinct from Sm'algyax (Coast Tsimshian) and from Haida, Tlingit, and Salishan. The Delgamuukw case is publicly documented context (Level 1); specific hereditary chief protocols and territory-specific details are Level 2-3. Do not aggregate with Wet'suwet'en despite their historical alliance and shared legal case.

name: Wet'suwet'en
language: Witsuwit'en (an Athabascan language — distinct from neighboring Tsimshianic and Salishan languages)
traditionalTerritories: Bulkley and Upper Morice River watersheds in north-central BC — interior plateau and mountain territory connected to the coast via the Skeena River system. Includes the Bulkley Valley around Smithers, BC.
culturalNotes: The Wet'suwet'en are an Athabascan-speaking people of the BC interior — culturally and linguistically distinct from their coastal and Tsimshianic neighbors. The Wet'suwet'en joined with the Gitxsan in the Delgamuukw case, establishing the evidentiary value of oral history and the persistence of Aboriginal title. The Wet'suwet'en hereditary chief system (House system with clan and house governance through hereditary title) became prominent in recent years through the Coastal GasLink pipeline conflict, in which hereditary chiefs and elected band council governance diverged — a distinction the heritage pack does not adjudicate. The Wet'suwet'en have maintained strong governance traditions through the House system despite colonial pressure.
heritageSkillRelevance: Bulkley River salmon fishing — significant chinook and coho runs in an interior Athabascan context
Interior plateau food traditions — root foods (camas in lower elevations), berries, moose and caribou
Witsuwit'en language revitalization programs
Snowshoe construction for interior plateau travel
Interior forest plant knowledge — distinct from coastal traditions
Birch bark canoe use in the upper Bulkley watershed
Smoking and drying salmon for interior winter storage
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Wet'suwet'en is an Athabascan language — not Wakashan, not Salishan, not Tsimshianic. The Delgamuukw legal context is publicly documented (Level 1); hereditary chief protocols and house governance are Level 2-3. The pack does not take positions on the hereditary chief vs. band council governance distinction — that is an internal governance matter. Do not aggregate with Gitxsan despite their alliance.

name: Nisga'a Nation
traditionalTerritories: Nass River valley in northern BC from the Nass headwaters to Portland Inlet. The Nass River is one of the major eulachon rivers on the BC coast.
culturalNotes: The Nisga'a Nation achieved the first modern treaty in BC (Nisga'a Final Agreement, 2000) after decades of political work that began with their 1973 lobbying effort in Ottawa. The Nisga'a case (Calder v. British Columbia, 1973) was the first Canadian Supreme Court case to recognize that Aboriginal title might exist in law — a foundational case for all subsequent Aboriginal title law in Canada. The Nass River eulachon runs are among the most culturally and economically significant on the coast — the 'grease trail' from the Nass River to interior Gitxsan and Athabascan peoples was a major trade corridor.
heritageSkillRelevance: Nass River eulachon (oolichan) harvesting and oil rendering — one of the most significant eulachon fisheries in BC
Grease trail trade network — connecting Nisga'a coast to interior Gitxsan, Wet'suwet'en, and Tahltan peoples
Nass River salmon fishing — significant chinook and sockeye runs
Nisga'a language revitalization (one of the better-resourced Tsimshianic language programs)
Dugout canoe construction for Nass River and Portland Inlet travel
Potlatch traditions — publicly shared gift economy framing (Level 1); ceremonial details are Level 3-4
Totem pole traditions — clan and family ownership of designs applies
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Nisga'a is a Tsimshianic language family — distinct from Haida, Tlingit, Wakashan, and Salishan. The Calder case and Nisga'a Final Agreement are publicly documented (Level 1). Ceremonial and potlatch protocols are Level 3-4. Do not aggregate with neighboring Tsimshian (Coast Tsimshian uses Sm'algyax; Nisga'a uses Nisg̱a'a).

name: Tahltan Nation
traditionalTerritories: Stikine, Tahltan, Iskut, and Klappan river watersheds in the northern BC interior and Yukon border region. Traditional territory extends from the interior plateau to the headwaters of the Stikine River system.
culturalNotes: The Tahltan are a hunting, fishing, and trading people of the northern BC interior. The Stikine River corridor connects Tahltan interior territory to the coast via the Alaska Panhandle, making the Tahltan important trade intermediaries between the coast (Tlingit) and the interior. The Tahltan dog — a now-extinct small hunting dog unique to the Tahltan — was one of the few dog breeds developed by any North American people for specific hunting purposes. Tahltan are currently among the most active First Nations opposing mining development in their territory (the 'Sacred Headwaters' of the Stikine, Skeena, and Nass rivers).
heritageSkillRelevance: Stikine River salmon fishing — significant chinook runs in the Stikine system connect coast to interior
Tahltan language revitalization efforts
Moose, caribou, and mountain goat hunting in BC northern interior
Trade network navigation — Stikine corridor between Tlingit coast and Tahltan interior
Snowshoe construction for northern plateau travel
Interior plant knowledge — high-elevation berry harvesting, medicinal plants
Birch bark canoe and river travel on Stikine headwaters
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Tahltan is an Athabascan language family people of the interior — not a coastal people, but connected to the Salish Sea watershed through the Stikine River salmon corridor. Do not aggregate with coastal peoples as 'Northwest Coast.' Ceremonial content is Level 3-4.
